title: One Sentence of Prayer
subtitle: A three-minute daily ritual of one short sentence, said in your own voice, to whatever you talk to when you talk
excerpt: A three-minute daily ritual. One sentence, said in your own voice, to whatever you talk to when you talk. Used as the smallest workable practice for people who want a spiritual habit and have not been able to make a longer one stick.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The short-prayer custom appears across many religious and spiritual traditions. The one-sentence form is also a feature of the breath-prayer custom in Christian and Orthodox writing.

practice.whenToUse: Use daily, ideally at the same point in the day. Workable as a morning practice with the first cup of tea, or in the car before the day starts.

content.text: A three-minute daily ritual. One sentence, said in your own voice, to whatever you talk to when you talk. The smallest workable form of a daily spiritual practice.
New to ritual as a practice? Read
Many people want a daily spiritual practice and have not made a longer one stick. The thirty-minute meditation does not survive the school run. The morning pages do not survive the bad night.
One sentence survives. It can be said in the car, at the kettle, on the way to the toilet. It does not need a cushion, a quiet room, or a clear hour.

Pick the sentence ahead of time. Same sentence every day for a week. "Thank you for this day." "Hold the people I love." "Help me move slowly today." "Let me see what is in front of me." The sentence is yours.
Pick the moment. Same moment every day. First sip of tea. Pulling out of the drive. Sitting down at the desk. The repetition is the practice.
When the moment comes, take one slow breath. Say the sentence, in your head or out loud. Take one slow breath after.
Then carry on with the next thing.
After a week, ask whether the sentence is still the right one. If yes, keep it. If no, write a new one and run it for another week.
Original to homemade.education. The short-prayer custom appears in many faiths. The one-sentence form is close to the breath-prayer custom in Christian and Orthodox writing.
